meta.name: Coroutine Runtime Engine
meta.description: Build an M:N user-space scheduler and coroutine runtime similar to Go's goroutines, implementing work-stealing, preemptive scheduling, and efficient context switching.
meta.essence: User-space threads (coroutines/fibers) scheduled onto kernel threads with work-stealing algorithms, stackful coroutine implementation, preemptive scheduling via timer or compiler cooperation, and efficient context switching without kernel involvement.
meta.why_important: Understanding coroutine runtimes is essential for building high-concurrency systems. This knowledge is valued at $200K-400K+ at companies building async runtimes, game engines, and high-performance servers.
learning_outcomes: Implement stackful coroutines with custom stacks
Handle preemptive scheduling without kernel support
Implement async I/O integration with event loop
Handle stack growth and overflow detection

prerequisites.name: Understanding of OS threading and scheduling
Assembly or inline assembly for context switch

milestones.name: Stackful Coroutines
milestones.description: Implement stackful coroutines with separate execution stacks and context switching between them.
acceptance_criteria: Each coroutine has its own stack (fixed or growable)
Context switch saves/restores all necessary registers
Coroutines can yield control voluntarily
Coroutines can return values on completion
Stack overflow detection implemented
Works on x86-64 (assembly or ucontext)
Basic benchmark shows context switch overhead
pitfalls: Floating point/SIMD register saving is expensive
ucontext is deprecated on some systems

milestones.name: Work-Stealing Scheduler
milestones.description: Implement an M:N scheduler with work-stealing for load balancing across kernel threads.
acceptance_criteria: Global queue for new coroutines
Per-thread local queues for cache locality
Work-stealing when local queue empty
Configurable number of worker threads
Random stealing to reduce contention
No deadlock under any scheduling pattern
Scaling efficiency > 70% up to CPU core count

milestones.name: Preemptive Scheduling
milestones.description: Implement preemptive scheduling to prevent runaway coroutines from starving other work.
acceptance_criteria: Coroutines can be preempted after time slice
Preemption implemented via timer or compiler cooperation
No need for coroutine cooperation (cooperative preemption)
Preemption points inserted at safe locations
Preemption overhead is minimal (< 1% of execution time)
Fair scheduling across all runnable coroutines
pitfalls: Preemption at wrong point corrupts state
Preemption during locks causes deadlock risk

milestones.name: Async I/O Integration
milestones.description: Integrate the coroutine scheduler with async I/O for efficient network and file operations without blocking kernel threads.
acceptance_criteria: Coroutines can await I/O without blocking threads
Integration with epoll/kqueue/io_uring
Socket operations (accept, read, write) are async
File operations (read, write) are async
Timer/coroutine sleep is efficient
I/O multiplexing scales to thousands of connections
Benchmarks show competitive throughput with epoll directly
pitfalls: Blocking I/O in coroutine blocks entire thread
File I/O is often blocking; need thread pool
Cancellation of pending I/O is complex

milestones.name: Synchronization Primitives & Polish
milestones.description: Implement coroutine-aware synchronization primitives and optimize the runtime for production use.
acceptance_criteria: Coroutine mutex (async-aware)
Coroutine condition variable
Semaphore and channel primitives
Deadlock detection or prevention
Runtime statistics (coroutine count, queue depth)
Graceful shutdown with coroutine drain
Performance within 2x of Go/Tokio on benchmarks
pitfalls: Regular mutex in coroutine defeats scheduling
Deadlock with coroutine primitives is easy

blueprint.title: Coroutine Runtime Engine
blueprint.overview:
Build a production-grade M:N user-space scheduler and coroutine runtime from scratch, implementing the same core mechanisms that power Go's goroutines, Rust's Tokio, and high-performance game engines. This project teaches you how to bridge the gap between hardware threads and software concurrency—how to give programs the illusion of unlimited parallelism while efficiently multiplexing onto a fixed number of kernel threads.

The core challenge is the impedance mismatch between what software wants (thousands or millions of concurrent tasks, instant switching, no blocking) and what hardware provides (4-128 CPU cores, expensive context switches, no awareness of application-level scheduling). A coroutine runtime negotiates this gap by moving thread management into user space, where switching costs nanoseconds instead of microseconds, and where the scheduler has semantic knowledge of what tasks are doing.

You'll implement stackful coroutines with custom stacks, a work-stealing scheduler that scales linearly with CPU count, preemptive scheduling to prevent runaway tasks from starving others, and async I/O integration so coroutines can wait for network/disk without blocking threads. This is the same architecture used by Go, C#'s async/await, and modern game engines.
blueprint.design_philosophy:
This project teaches systems thinking at its purest: every design decision is a negotiation between hardware constraints and software desires. You'll face the same tradeoffs that language runtime teams debate: Should stacks be fixed-size or growable? Should preemption be timer-based or compiler-assisted? Should work-stealing be random or locality-aware? There's no single right answer—only tradeoffs that shift based on workload characteristics.

The progression is deliberate: Milestone 1 (coroutines) teaches you how to switch execution contexts without kernel involvement. Milestone 2 (work-stealing) teaches you how to distribute work across threads. Milestone 3 (preemption) teaches you how to maintain fairness without cooperation. Milestone 4 (async I/O) teaches you how to integrate with the OS event loop. Milestone 5 (synchronization) teaches you how to make the whole thing production-ready. Each milestone unlocks the next, and together they form a complete mental model of how modern async runtimes work.

implementation.rationale: Rust is the ideal choice for a coroutine runtime because: (1) Zero-cost abstractions mean you pay nothing for safety features you don't use—critical for context switching hot paths. (2) The borrow checker enforces the exact discipline needed for safe coroutine scheduling: no data races across suspended coroutines, no use-after-free in stack management. (3) No garbage collector means coroutine stacks are predictable and can't trigger GC pauses. (4) Native async/await support provides a reference implementation to compare against. (5) The unsafe escape hatch allows raw pointer manipulation for context switching while keeping the rest of the runtime safe. C is also viable but lacks the safety guarantees; C++ has the capabilities but RAII makes stack management trickier.
implementation.style_guide: Use snake_case for functions/variables, PascalCase for types. Prefer Result<T, E> over panics in library code. Mark unsafe blocks with // SAFETY: comments explaining invariants. Use cfg(target_arch) for platform-specific assembly. Prefer const generics over macros for sized types. Document all public APIs with /// doc comments including safety requirements and examples.
implementation.build_system: Cargo with workspace structure. Main crate + benches/ for criterion benchmarks + fuzz/ for scheduler fuzzing. Profile.release settings: lto = 'thin', codegen-units = 1, debug = 1 for profiling.
alternatives: C — More explicit control, but no borrow checker means coroutine state bugs become runtime crashes. Better for learning raw mechanics, worse for building something production-ready.
C++ — Similar capabilities to Rust, but RAII destructors complicate stack switching. boost::context exists as a reference.
Zig — Excellent for this project: explicit allocators, comptime for platform abstraction, no hidden control flow. Slightly less mature ecosystem.

assumed_known: Pointer arithmetic and memory layout (struct padding, alignment)
Basic threading (spawn, join, mutex) in any language
Understanding of stack vs heap allocation
How function calls work (stack frames, return addresses)
Reading x86-64 assembly (not writing from scratch)

must_teach_first.concept: Stack alignment requirements (16-byte on x86-64 System V ABI)

milestones.title: Stackful Coroutines
milestones.summary: Build the fundamental unit of execution in the runtime: a coroutine with its own stack that can be suspended and resumed. You'll implement context switching in assembly, manage stack allocation, and create the yield/resume API that everything else builds upon.
milestones.misconception: Developers often think context switching requires kernel involvement—that only the OS can save and restore thread state. In reality, a context switch is just saving some registers to memory and loading others. The kernel does this for threads, but you can do it for coroutines in user space with ~10x lower overhead.
milestones.reveal: A context switch is just a function call with extra steps. You save the instruction pointer, stack pointer, and callee-saved registers to a struct, then load another struct's values back. The CPU doesn't know or care that you've switched logical threads—it's just executing instructions. The magic is in the discipline: save exactly the registers the ABI requires, maintain stack alignment, and never corrupt the red zone.
cascade: Green threads in Go/Java — same mechanism, different scheduling policies. Once you understand stackful coroutines, goroutines are just coroutines with a runtime scheduler.
Async/await transformation (cross-domain) — compiler converts async functions into state machines that store their 'registers' in a struct. Same concept, different implementation strategy.
Interrupt handlers in kernels — CPU does hardware context switch on interrupt. Your coroutine switch is the software analog, with the same save/restore pattern.
Fibers in Windows/game engines — Microsoft's fiber API is literally this. Game engines use fibers for frame-based task scheduling.
Continuation-passing style (cross-domain) — in functional languages, continuations capture the 'rest of computation.' A suspended coroutine is a stored continuation.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Each coroutine has its own stack (fixed or growable)
Context switch saves/restores all necessary registers
Coroutines can yield control voluntarily
Coroutines can return values on completion
Stack overflow detection implemented
Works on x86-64 (assembly or ucontext)
Basic benchmark shows context switch overhead

milestones.title: Work-Stealing Scheduler
milestones.summary: Implement the M:N scheduler that multiplexes thousands of coroutines onto a fixed pool of kernel threads. You'll build work-stealing queues that let idle threads steal work from busy ones, achieving near-linear scaling up to CPU core count.
milestones.misconception: Developers often believe more threads = more parallelism. But kernel threads are expensive (1-2MB stack, ~1μs context switch, scheduler contention). The key insight is M:N scheduling: M coroutines on N threads where M >> N. The runtime, not the OS, decides which coroutine runs on which thread.
milestones.reveal: Work-stealing is provably optimal for fork-join parallelism. When a thread's local queue is empty, it doesn't wait—it steals from another thread's queue. This creates automatic load balancing: threads with more work naturally share with threads with less. The math shows expected time is O(T1/P + T∞) where T1 is total work, P is processors, and T∞ is critical path length. You can't do better than that.
cascade: MapReduce/Spark schedulers (cross-domain) — same work-stealing principle applied to distributed data processing. A slow node's work gets stolen by faster nodes.
Go scheduler's 'runq' — exact same design: per-P local queues, global queue for overflow, random stealing. Your implementation mirrors production systems.
Job systems in game engines — AAA games use work-stealing for parallel rendering/physics. Same algorithm, different domain constraints.
Lock-free algorithms — work-stealing queues are a masterclass in lock-free design. Chase-Lev queues use atomic indices and careful memory ordering.
NUMA-aware scheduling (advanced) — once you understand work-stealing, you can extend it to steal from NUMA-local queues first, reducing cross-socket traffic.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Global queue for new coroutines
Per-thread local queues for cache locality
Work-stealing when local queue empty
Configurable number of worker threads
Random stealing to reduce contention
No deadlock under any scheduling pattern
Scaling efficiency > 70% up to CPU core count

milestones.title: Preemptive Scheduling
milestones.summary: Implement preemptive scheduling so that CPU-bound coroutines can't starve others. You'll use timer signals or compiler-inserted yield points to force coroutines to yield after a time slice, ensuring fairness across all runnable work.
milestones.misconception: Developers assume cooperative scheduling (coroutines yield voluntarily) is sufficient. But one badly-written CPU-bound loop blocks an entire thread. Without preemption, you can't provide latency guarantees, and debugging is nightmare: the system 'freezes' because one coroutine is stuck in a tight loop.
milestones.reveal: Preemption in user space is a controlled violation of the coroutine abstraction. You interrupt execution at a safe point, save state, and resume later. Go does this by checking a 'preempt' flag on function entry. The runtime sets the flag from a signal handler; the coroutine checks it at every function call. It's cooperative at the micro-scale (yield points) but preemptive at the macro-scale (time slices).
cascade: Operating system schedulers — kernel preemption follows the same pattern: timer interrupt → save state → choose next task → restore. Your preemption is the user-space analog.
Garbage collection safe points (cross-domain) — GC languages need to pause threads at safe points. Same concept: compiler inserts check points, runtime triggers pause.
Debuggers and breakpoints — interrupting execution to inspect state. Your preemption mechanism is like a debugger that automatically 'breaks' on timeout.
Real-time systems — hard real-time requires bounded preemption latency. Understanding preemption is essential for real-time scheduling.

Cooperative multitasking history — Windows 3.1/Mac OS 9 were entirely cooperative. One bad app froze the system. Preemptive scheduling solved this at the OS level; you're solving it at the coroutine level.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Coroutines can be preempted after time slice
Preemption implemented via timer or compiler cooperation
No need for coroutine cooperation (cooperative preemption)
Preemption points inserted at safe locations
Preemption overhead is minimal (< 1% of execution time)
Fair scheduling across all runnable coroutines

milestones.title: Async I/O Integration
milestones.summary: Integrate the coroutine scheduler with the operating system's async I/O facilities (epoll/kqueue/io_uring). When a coroutine awaits I/O, the thread doesn't block—it runs other coroutines. When I/O completes, the coroutine is rescheduled.
milestones.misconception: Developers think 'async I/O' means non-blocking syscalls. But most file I/O on Linux is blocking regardless of O_NONBLOCK. True async requires io_uring or thread pools. And even with non-blocking sockets, you need to integrate with the OS event notification system—otherwise you're polling, which wastes CPU.
milestones.reveal: The key insight is the 'reactor pattern': a single thread calls epoll_wait/kqueue to block until any file descriptor is ready. Coroutines register interest in an fd, then yield. When epoll returns, the reactor wakes the appropriate coroutine. The thread never blocks on I/O—it only blocks when there's no work at all. This is how Nginx handles 10K+ connections with a few threads.
cascade: Node.js event loop (cross-domain) — exact same reactor pattern. libuv provides the cross-platform abstraction. Your implementation is the systems-level view of what Node provides.
Redis networking — single-threaded but handles 100K+ QPS because of efficient epoll integration. One thread, many connections, event-driven.
Nginx/Apache event modules — production web servers use this pattern. Understanding it unlocks high-performance server design.
io_uring (advanced) — next-generation Linux async I/O. Submission/completion queues replace epoll. Same conceptual model, better performance.
Database connection pools (cross-domain) — same pattern: check out connection, issue query, yield, wake on response. Connection pools are coroutine-aware in modern frameworks.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Coroutines can await I/O without blocking threads
Integration with epoll/kqueue/io_uring
Socket operations (accept, read, write) are async
File operations (read, write) are async
Timer/coroutine sleep is efficient
I/O multiplexing scales to thousands of connections
Benchmarks show competitive throughput with epoll directly

milestones.title: Synchronization Primitives & Polish
milestones.summary: Build coroutine-aware synchronization primitives (mutex, condition variable, channels) and production features (runtime statistics, graceful shutdown). These primitives yield instead of blocking, keeping threads productive even when coroutines wait.
milestones.misconception: Developers reach for std::sync::Mutex or pthread_mutex in coroutines. This blocks the entire thread, defeating M:N scheduling. If a thread is blocked on a mutex, all other coroutines on that thread starze. Coroutine-aware primitives must yield instead of blocking.
milestones.reveal: A coroutine mutex doesn't block—it queues waiters and yields the current coroutine. When the mutex is released, a waiter is woken. The thread continues executing other coroutines. This is why async Rust has std::sync::Mutex vs tokio::sync::Mutex—they look similar but have radically different semantics. The async version never blocks a thread.
cascade: Deadlock detection (cross-domain) — same algorithms apply to coroutine deadlocks. Wait-for graphs, cycle detection, timeout-based recovery.
Go channels (cross-domain) — channels are coroutine-aware queues. send() yields if buffer full; recv() yields if empty. Your implementation mirrors Go's design.
Distributed consensus (cross-domain) — Paxos/Raft require careful synchronization. Understanding local sync primitives is a prerequisite for distributed sync.
Lock-free data structures — channels can be lock-free (MPSC queues). Your channel implementation touches on lock-free design.
Runtime observability — production runtimes expose metrics (goroutine count, GC pause time). Your stats collection mirrors production monitoring.
yaml_acceptance_criteria: Coroutine mutex (async-aware)
Coroutine condition variable
Semaphore and channel primitives
Deadlock detection or prevention
Runtime statistics (coroutine count, queue depth)
Graceful shutdown with coroutine drain
Performance within 2x of Go/Tokio on benchmarks

diagrams.title: Coroutine Runtime Architecture (Satellite View)
diagrams.description: The complete runtime from 30,000 feet: application coroutines at the top, scheduler in the middle, kernel threads at the bottom, I/O reactor on the side. Shows data flow from coroutine creation through scheduling to execution to I/O wait. Every subsequent diagram zooms into one box on this map.

diagrams.title: x86-64 Calling Convention & Register Classes
diagrams.description: Visual reference for which registers must be saved by caller vs callee. Shows the 6 argument registers, callee-saved set (rbx, rbp, r12-r15), and special registers (rsp, rip). Critical reference for implementing context switch correctly.

diagrams.title: Coroutine Stack Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte-level view of a coroutine stack: guard page at bottom (overflow detection), stack proper growing upward, current frame at top. Shows alignment requirements (16-byte), red zone (128 bytes), and how to detect overflow via guard page SIGSEGV.

diagrams.title: Context Switch State Machine
diagrams.description: Step-by-step trace of a yield operation: save current registers to Coroutine A's context struct, load Coroutine B's context, jump to B's resume point. Shows both the assembly instructions and the abstract state transitions.

diagrams.title: Coroutine Control Block Structure
diagrams.description: Memory layout of the Coroutine struct: context (saved registers), stack pointer, stack size, state enum (Created/Running/Suspended/Finished), return value slot. Shows Rust struct definition with field offsets.

diagrams.title: Yield/Resume Sequence Diagram
diagrams.description: Time-sequence view: main creates coroutine, resumes it, coroutine executes then yields, main gets control back, main resumes again, coroutine completes. Shows return values flowing back and forth.

diagrams.title: Stack Growth Strategies: Fixed vs Segmented vs Contiguous
diagrams.description: Three approaches to stack management: (1) Fixed-size: simple but wastes memory or risks overflow. (2) Segmented: linked list of chunks, complex but flexible. (3) Contiguous: mremap to grow, requires OS support. Go switched from segmented to contiguous in 1.4.

diagrams.title: Context Switch Assembly Annotated
diagrams.description: Line-by-line annotated x86-64 assembly for context switch. Each instruction paired with its purpose: 'mov [rdi+0], rbx — save rbx to context struct at offset 0'. Shows both the switch_to and switch_from entry points.

diagrams.title: M:N Scheduler Architecture
diagrams.description: The big picture: M coroutines managed by N kernel threads. Shows the global run queue, per-thread local queues, and work-stealing arrows between threads. Illustrates why M >> N is efficient (less kernel overhead, better cache locality).

diagrams.title: Chase-Lev Work-Stealing Queue Internals
diagrams.description: The deque data structure that enables work-stealing: owner pushes/pops from bottom (LIFO for cache locality), thieves steal from top (FIFO for fairness). Shows atomic indices, circular buffer, and the memory ordering required for correctness.

diagrams.title: Work-Stealing Race Conditions
diagrams.description: What can go wrong: owner and thief both try to take the last item. Shows the atomic compare-and-swap that resolves this, and the CAS loop that handles contention. Illustrates why random stealing reduces contention.

diagrams.title: Scheduler State Machine
diagrams.description: Worker thread lifecycle: start → poll local queue → (empty? steal) → (still empty? park) → (wake? poll again). Shows transitions between running, stealing, and parked states.

diagrams.title: Scaling Efficiency vs Thread Count
diagrams.description: Graph showing ideal linear scaling vs actual with work-stealing. Shows where contention kicks in (usually 2x core count), the impact of random vs directed stealing, and NUMA effects at high thread counts.

diagrams.title: Global Queue Bottleneck Analysis
diagrams.description: Why the global queue is a bottleneck: all threads contend on it for new coroutines. Shows lock contention under load, and how per-thread local queues reduce this to near-zero for steady-state workloads.

diagrams.title: Load Balancing Under Skewed Workloads
diagrams.description: Three scenarios: (1) All work on one thread, others steal. (2) Fork-join pattern, work spreads then converges. (3) Producer-consumer, work flows from producer threads to consumer threads. Shows how work-stealing handles each.

diagrams.title: Preemption Mechanisms Compared
diagrams.description: Three approaches side-by-side: (1) Timer signal (SIGUSR1) — preempts anywhere, but signal safety is hard. (2) Compiler yield points — safe but requires instrumentation. (3) Hybrid — timer sets flag, yield points check it. Shows trade-offs of each.

diagrams.title: Signal Handler Execution Flow
diagrams.description: What happens when timer fires: signal interrupts coroutine, handler saves additional state, sets preempt flag, returns. Coroutine checks flag at next yield point, invokes scheduler. Shows the split between 'interrupt' and 'actual switch'.

diagrams.title: Safe Points for Preemption
diagrams.description: Where is it safe to preempt? Function entry (stack is consistent), loop back-edges (loop invariants hold), allocation points (GC-safe). Shows unsafe points: inside lock, mid-struct-update, FFI boundary.

diagrams.title: Time Slice Scheduling Visualization
diagrams.description: Timeline showing three coroutines with different work amounts. Without preemption: A runs forever. With preemption: all three get time slices, fair distribution. Shows how time slice duration affects latency vs throughput.

diagrams.title: Preemption Overhead Breakdown
diagrams.description: Where does the <1% overhead come from? Signal delivery (~100ns), flag check on function entry (~1ns), occasional context switch (~50ns). Shows that overhead is negligible compared to useful work.

diagrams.title: Reactor Pattern Architecture
diagrams.description: The reactor loop: epoll_wait blocks until events ready → for each ready fd, wake corresponding coroutine → scheduler runs woken coroutines → coroutines yield on next I/O → reactor waits again. Shows the cycle of block-process-wake-yield.

diagrams.title: epoll State Machine
diagrams.description: Lifecycle of an async socket read: coroutine calls async_read → register fd with epoll → yield → reactor gets EPOLLIN → wake coroutine → coroutine reads data → return to caller. Shows state transitions.

diagrams.title: Blocking I/O vs Async I/O Timeline
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: blocking thread waits for each I/O, 90% idle. Async thread processes other coroutines during I/O wait, near 100% utilization. Shows throughput improvement.

diagrams.title: File I/O Thread Pool Architecture
diagrams.description: Why regular file I/O needs a thread pool on Linux: O_NONBLOCK doesn't work for disk. Shows the blocking-pool pattern: coroutine submits file op to pool → pool thread blocks on read → completion wakes coroutine.

diagrams.title: io_uring: The Next Generation
diagrams.description: How io_uring differs from epoll: submission queue (app → kernel), completion queue (kernel → app), no syscalls per operation. Shows the shared memory ring buffers and the batched submission/completion model.

diagrams.title: Timer Wheel for Coroutine Sleep
diagrams.description: How to implement efficient sleep(timeout): timer wheel data structure with O(1) insert, O(1) tick. Shows the hierarchical timing wheel for handling timeouts from milliseconds to hours.

diagrams.title: Scaling to 10K+ Connections
diagrams.description: Resource usage comparison: thread-per-connection (10K threads = 10GB+ stack memory) vs coroutine-per-connection (10K coroutines = 10MB stack memory with 4KB stacks). Shows why coroutines enable massive concurrency.

diagrams.title: Async Mutex vs Blocking Mutex
diagrams.description: Critical difference: blocking mutex blocks thread (all coroutines on thread stall). Async mutex yields coroutine (thread runs other coroutines). Shows the internal queue of waiting coroutines in async mutex.

diagrams.title: MPSC Channel Internals
diagrams.description: Multi-producer single-consumer channel: sender queues (lock-free), receiver deque, buffer or unbounded. Shows the atomic operations for send/recv and how receivers wake on send.

diagrams.title: Async Condition Variable Pattern
diagrams.description: How to implement wait/signal for coroutines: wait() adds coroutine to wait list and yields. signal() removes one coroutine and schedules it. broadcast() schedules all waiters. Shows the wait queue structure.

diagrams.title: Deadlock Detection via Wait-For Graph
diagrams.description: How to detect deadlocks: build wait-for graph (coroutine A waits for mutex held by B), detect cycles. Shows graph construction and cycle detection algorithm. Optional: timeout-based recovery.

diagrams.title: Runtime Statistics Dashboard
diagrams.description: What metrics to track: coroutine count by state, queue depths per thread, steal attempts/successes, preemption count, I/O wait count. Shows how these metrics reveal performance issues.

diagrams.title: Graceful Shutdown Sequence
diagrams.description: How to shut down cleanly: stop accepting new coroutines → drain running coroutines → cancel pending I/O → join worker threads → free resources. Shows the state machine for shutdown.

diagrams.title: Performance vs Go/Tokio Benchmarks
diagrams.description: Side-by-side benchmark results: context switch latency, scheduler throughput, I/O ops/sec, memory per coroutine. Shows where your implementation is competitive and where there's room for improvement.

diagrams.title: Hardware View: CPU Cores, Hyperthreads, Cache
diagrams.description: The hardware reality: L1 cache per core, L2 per core or shared, L3 shared. Hyperthreads share L1/L2. Shows why work-stealing across hyperthreads is faster than across sockets (NUMA).

diagrams.title: Memory Layout: 10K Coroutines
diagrams.description: What 10,000 coroutines looks like in memory: coroutine structs (heap), stacks (mmap'd regions), scheduler queues. Shows total memory usage and cache efficiency implications.

diagrams.title: Architecture Comparison: Your Runtime vs Go vs Tokio
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of three runtimes: Go (segmented stacks → contiguous, work-stealing, netpoll), Tokio (stackless coroutines, work-stealing, mio-based reactor), Your runtime (design choices). Shows where they converge and differ.

tdd_blueprint.design_vision: Build a production-grade M:N user-space scheduler and coroutine runtime implementing stackful coroutines with custom stacks, a work-stealing scheduler for linear scalability, preemptive scheduling for fairness, async I/O integration with epoll/kqueue, and coroutine-aware synchronization primitives. The runtime bridges the gap between hardware threads (4-128 cores) and software concurrency demands (thousands of concurrent tasks), achieving context switches in ~20-50ns vs ~1-2μs for kernel threads, enabling efficient handling of 10,000+ concurrent connections.

modules.name: Stackful Coroutines
modules.description: Implements the fundamental execution unit with its own stack, capable of being suspended and resumed. Each coroutine has a 64KB stack with guard page protection. Does NOT include scheduling logic or preemption—only the coroutine primitive and context switching mechanism.

specs.inputs: Entry function (FnOnce closure), stack size configuration, initial coroutine state
specs.outputs: Coroutine control block with saved context, state transitions (Runnable/Running/Finished/Panicked), return value on completion
specs.abstractions: Coroutine struct wrapping stack allocation, Context struct for saved CPU state, Stack struct with guard page support, thread-local current coroutine tracking
error_categories: Context corruption (invalid register state on resume)
Panic across FFI boundary (unwinding through assembly)
specs.concurrency_model: Single-threaded per coroutine execution. Thread-local storage tracks current coroutine. Context switches are synchronous function calls from caller's perspective.
performance_targets: Memory per coroutine: 64KB stack + ~200 bytes control block
Guard page fault handling: < 1μs to signal handler
syscall_list: mmap/mprotect for stack allocation with guard page (direct or via alloc)
sigaction for stack overflow handler installation

memory_layout.Coroutine struct: id(8) + state(1) + padding(7) + context(64) + stack.ptr(8) + stack.size(8) + entry(16) = ~112 bytes, fits in 2 cache lines
memory_layout.Context struct: regs[6](48) + rsp(8) + rip(8) = 64 bytes, exactly 1 cache line
memory_layout.Stack: 64KB contiguous, page-aligned, first page PROT_NONE as guard

specs.cache_line_analysis: Context struct is hot on every switch (64 bytes = 1 cache line). Stack is cold when suspended. Coroutine metadata read on every schedule decision.

implementation_phases.name: Coroutine control block and state machine
Entry point trampoline and exit handling
Stack overflow detection and signal handler

diagrams.title: Coroutine Control Block Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte-level layout of Coroutine struct showing all fields, padding, and cache line boundaries. Annotates hot vs cold fields and expected access patterns.

diagrams.title: Context Struct Register Mapping
diagrams.description: 64-byte Context struct showing mapping from struct offsets to x86-64 registers (rbx, rbp, r12-r15, rsp, rip). Includes alignment verification.

diagrams.title: Stack Memory Layout with Guard Page
diagrams.description: 64KB stack region showing guard page (PROT_NONE), usable stack space, growth direction, and stack top pointer calculation.

diagrams.title: Context Switch Assembly Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step walkthrough of context_switch assembly: save callee-saved registers to from->regs[], save rsp, save rip from [rsp], load to->regs, load rsp, jmp to rip.

diagrams.title: Coroutine State Machine
diagrams.description: States (Runnable, Running, Finished, Panicked, Blocked) and valid transitions. Shows which transitions happen in which functions.

diagrams.title: Coroutine Spawn Sequence
diagrams.description: From Coroutine::spawn() call through stack allocation, context initialization, entry point setup, to returning ready-to-run coroutine.

diagrams.title: Yield/Resume Round Trip
diagrams.description: Sequence from yield_now() call through context save, scheduler switch, and eventual resume back to yield point.

diagrams.title: Initial Stack Frame Setup
diagrams.description: How Context::new() sets up the initial stack: coroutine_ptr argument, fake return address (coroutine_exit), alignment to 16n+8.

diagrams.title: Entry Trampoline Flow
diagrams.description: coroutine_entry trampoline: receive coroutine_ptr, set state to Running, take entry closure, call with catch_unwind, set final state.

diagrams.title: Stack Overflow Detection Path
diagrams.description: From write to guard page through SIGSEGV delivery to signal handler to coroutine state update.

diagrams.title: x86-64 Callee-Saved Registers
diagrams.description: Visual reference of x86-64 System V ABI register classes: caller-saved (rax, rcx, rdx, rsi, rdi, r8-r11) vs callee-saved (rbx, rbp, r12-r15).

diagrams.title: Stack Alignment Requirements
diagrams.description: Demonstrates 16-byte alignment requirement: before CALL (16n), after CALL (16n+8), function entry after push rbp (16n).

modules.name: Work-Stealing Scheduler
modules.description: Implements M:N scheduling with Chase-Lev work-stealing queues. Each worker thread has a local queue (LIFO for pops), with a global queue for new coroutines and overflow. Idle workers steal from random victims (FIFO from their queue end). Targets >70% scaling efficiency up to CPU core count.

specs.inputs: Configurable number of worker threads, spawned coroutines, work-stealing requests
specs.outputs: Coroutines scheduled fairly across threads, scaling efficiency metrics, steal statistics
specs.abstractions: Scheduler with worker pool, Chase-Lev work-stealing queue per worker, global overflow queue, Condvar for idle worker wakeup
error_categories: Deadlock (all coroutines blocked, none can wake)
Thread starvation (one thread overloaded while others idle)
specs.concurrency_model: Each worker thread runs independent scheduling loop. Local queue operations are lock-free (Chase-Lev). Global queue uses mutex. Stealing is non-blocking CAS-based.
performance_targets: Scaling efficiency: >70% up to CPU core count
Zero deadlock under any scheduling pattern
syscall_list: futex (via Condvar) for idle worker wakeup

memory_layout.WorkStealingQueue: bottom(8) + pad(56) + top(8) + pad(56) + buffer_ptr(8) = 136 bytes, padded to avoid false sharing between top and bottom
memory_layout.Scheduler: workers mutex + global_queue mutex + local_queues[] + condvar + atomic flags, shared across all workers

specs.cache_line_analysis: Chase-Lev queue's bottom (owner) and top (thieves) on separate cache lines prevents false sharing. Global queue mutex is contention hotspot under high spawn rate.

implementation_phases.name: Stealing algorithm with random victim selection

diagrams.title: M:N Scheduler Architecture
diagrams.description: Global view showing M coroutines, N worker threads, global queue, per-thread local queues, and steal paths between workers.

diagrams.title: Chase-Lev Queue Internal State
diagrams.description: Circular buffer with bottom and top indices, showing empty state, partially filled, and full conditions. LIFO pop from bottom, FIFO steal from top.

diagrams.title: Chase-Lev Queue Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte offsets for bottom, padding, top, padding, buffer pointer. Annotates cache line boundaries and which threads access which fields.

diagrams.title: Push Operation Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: load bottom, load top, check capacity, grow if needed, write to buffer[b], fence(Release), store bottom+1.

diagrams.title: Pop Operation Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: load bottom-1, store bottom tentatively, fence(SeqCst), load top, handle empty case, handle last-item race with CAS.

diagrams.title: Steal Operation Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: load top (Acquire), fence(SeqCst), load bottom, check empty, read buffer[t], CAS top to top+1.

diagrams.title: Pop vs Steal Race Condition
diagrams.description: Shows the race when queue has 1 item: owner's pop and thief's steal both try to claim it. CAS resolves the race.

diagrams.title: Worker Thread State Machine
diagrams.description: States: FindingWork, RunningCoroutine, Spinning, Yielding, Sleeping, ShuttingDown. Transitions triggered by work availability and shutdown flag.

diagrams.title: find_work Priority Sequence
diagrams.description: Decision tree: local queue pop → if empty, global queue → if empty, steal from random victim → if all empty, spin/yield/sleep.

diagrams.title: Work-Stealing Under Load Imbalance
diagrams.description: Shows Thread A with 100 tasks, Thread B empty. Steal transfers 50 tasks. Both queues balanced.

diagrams.title: Random Victim Selection Flow
diagrams.description: PRNG generates victim index, check victim != self, attempt steal, if fail try next victim in round-robin.

diagrams.title: Scaling Efficiency Measurement
diagrams.description: Graph showing expected vs actual speedup from 1 to N threads. 70% efficiency line marked. Overhead breakdown.

diagrams.title: Global Queue Contention Scenario
diagrams.description: All threads trying to access global queue simultaneously. Shows mutex contention path and cache line bouncing.

diagrams.title: Idle Worker Wakeup Sequence
diagrams.description: From spawn() through notify_one() to sleeping worker waking via Condvar.

modules.name: Preemptive Scheduling
modules.description: Implements fair scheduling via timer-based preemption. A per-thread timer sets a preemption flag, which coroutines check at safe points (function entry, explicit yield). Achieves bounded latency with <1% overhead. Time slice is configurable (default 10ms).

specs.inputs: Timer configuration (time slice), preemption flag from signal handler
specs.outputs: Fair scheduling across all runnable coroutines, bounded latency per coroutine, preemption statistics
specs.abstractions: PreemptionState per thread (flag, last_preempt_time, timeslices_consumed), timer_create with SIGEV_THREAD_ID, signal handler, cooperative_yield function
specs.concurrency_model: Timer fires independently per thread. Signal handler sets atomic flag. Coroutine checks flag at safe points. No locks in signal handler path.
performance_targets: Flag check cost: <3 cycles per check
syscall_list: timer_create with SIGEV_THREAD_ID for per-thread timer

memory_layout.PREEMPTION_FLAG: AtomicBool padded to 64 bytes to avoid false sharing with other hot data

specs.cache_line_analysis: Preemption flag is read by coroutine (every safe point) and written by signal handler (once per time slice). Usually in shared cache state. Write causes brief cache bounce but acceptable due to low frequency.

diagrams.title: Preemption Mechanisms Comparison
diagrams.description: Three approaches side-by-side: signal-based (timer→SIGALRM→handler), compiler-assisted (yield points at function entry), hybrid (timer sets flag, check at safe points).

diagrams.title: Timer Signal Delivery Flow
diagrams.description: From timer_create through timer firing to signal handler execution on specific thread's stack.

diagrams.title: Preemption Flag State Machine
diagrams.description: Flag states: Clear, Set (by signal), Checked (by coroutine), Cleared (after yield).

diagrams.title: Safe Points for Preemption
diagrams.description: Code locations marked as safe: function entry, loop backedge start, after yield_now(), before blocking operations.

diagrams.title: Unsafe Preemption Points
diagrams.description: Code locations that must NOT be preempted: inside malloc/free, lock acquire/release, mid-expression, FFI boundary.

diagrams.title: cooperative_yield Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: check flag, if set then clear and yield_now(), else continue.

diagrams.title: Time Slice Fairness Visualization
diagrams.description: Timeline showing multiple coroutines each getting 10ms slices before preemption. Shows fairness guarantee.

diagrams.title: Preemption Overhead Breakdown
diagrams.description: Cost breakdown: flag check (2 cycles), signal delivery (50-100 cycles when fires), context switch (20-50ns). Total <1% overhead.

diagrams.title: Signal Handler Restrictions
diagrams.description: List of async-signal-safe operations (atomic store) vs unsafe (malloc, printf, mutex lock).

diagrams.title: Preemption During Lock Hold Scenario
diagrams.description: Problem scenario: coroutine holds mutex, gets preempted, other coroutines blocked waiting. Solution: don't preempt if locks_held > 0.

modules.name: Async I/O Integration
modules.description: Integrates coroutine scheduler with OS async I/O via epoll (Linux) for sockets and thread pool for file I/O. Implements reactor pattern with timer wheel for sleep(). Achieves scalability to 10,000+ concurrent connections with throughput within 2x of raw epoll.

specs.inputs: Non-blocking socket operations, file I/O requests, timer/sleep requests
specs.outputs: I/O completion notifications, woken coroutines, timer expirations
specs.abstractions: Reactor with epoll fd and waker map, AsyncTcpStream/AsyncTcpListener wrappers, FileIoPool for blocking file ops, TimerWheel for efficient sleep
specs.concurrency_model: Single reactor thread calls epoll_wait when no runnable coroutines. Multiple I/O threads in pool for file operations. Timer wheel processed on each reactor poll.
performance_targets: Echo server throughput within 2x of raw epoll
syscall_list: preadv/pwritev for file I/O (in thread pool)

memory_layout.Reactor: epoll_fd(4) + wakers HashMap + next_token atomic, shared across scheduler
memory_layout.TimerWheel: slots array (64-256 entries) + base time + current_slot, each slot is VecDeque<TimerEntry>

specs.cache_line_analysis: epoll_event array passed to epoll_wait is kernel-written. Waker map is mutex-protected, accessed on registration and event delivery. Timer wheel slots accessed on tick.

implementation_phases.name: poll() with timeout and event dispatch

diagrams.title: Reactor Pattern Architecture
diagrams.description: Reactor in center, coroutines on left registering interest, epoll_wait at bottom, wakers dispatching back to scheduler.

diagrams.title: epoll Event Flow
diagrams.description: From coroutine read() call through EAGAIN, register with reactor, yield, epoll_wait return, waker.wake(), resume.

diagrams.title: AsyncTcpStream State Machine
diagrams.description: States: Connecting, Connected, Reading, Writing, Closed. Transitions on epoll events.

diagrams.title: Non-blocking Read Algorithm
diagrams.description: Loop: try recv(), if WouldBlock then register for EPOLLIN and yield, else return bytes.

diagrams.title: File I/O Thread Pool Architecture
diagrams.description: Main thread submits to channel, worker threads dequeue and execute preadv/pwritev, waker wakes coroutine on completion.

diagrams.title: Timer Wheel Structure
diagrams.description: Circular array of slots, each containing VecDeque of TimerEntry. Current slot pointer advances on tick.

diagrams.title: Timer Wheel Tick Algorithm
diagrams.description: Process current slot: for each entry, if expired wake else refile to future slot. Advance pointer. Return time to next timer.

diagrams.title: Level vs Edge Triggered Semantics
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: level-triggered fires while data available, edge-triggered fires only on state change.

diagrams.title: 10K Connection Resource Analysis
diagrams.description: Per-connection: 64KB stack + 128KB recv buffer + 128KB send buffer + epoll entry. Total ~3.2GB for 10K.

diagrams.title: Reactor-Scheduler Integration
diagrams.description: Worker loop: find_work → if none, reactor.poll_with_timers() → process events → loop back.

diagrams.title: io_uring Alternative Architecture
diagrams.description: Comparison: submission queue (user→kernel) and completion queue (kernel→user) vs epoll's readiness notification.

diagrams.title: Socket Tuning for High Concurrency
diagrams.description: setrecv_buffer_size, set_send_buffer_size, set_nodelay, SO_REUSEADDR settings for 10K+ connections.

modules.name: Synchronization Primitives & Polish
modules.description: Implements coroutine-aware synchronization primitives (AsyncMutex, AsyncCondvar, MPSC channel, AsyncSemaphore) that yield instead of block. Adds runtime statistics, deadlock detection, graceful shutdown, and performance optimization to achieve within 2x of Go/Tokio benchmarks.

specs.inputs: Lock requests, channel send/recv, semaphore acquire, stats queries, shutdown signal
specs.outputs: Mutex guards, channel messages, semaphore permits, stats snapshots, shutdown completion
specs.abstractions: AsyncMutex with wait queue, AsyncCondvar with waiters, MPSC channel with bounded buffer, AsyncSemaphore with permits, DeadlockDetector with wait-for graph, RuntimeStats with atomics
specs.concurrency_model: All primitives use coroutine yielding, never thread blocking. Wait queues are mutex-protected. Wake operations schedule coroutines via scheduler reference.
performance_targets: Within 2x of Go/Tokio on all benchmarks
syscall_list: futex (avoided - these primitives don't use it)
Only thread management syscalls via scheduler

memory_layout.AsyncMutex: locked AtomicBool + waiters Mutex<VecDeque> + data UnsafeCell<T>
memory_layout.ChannelInner: queue Mutex<VecDeque> + recv_waiters + send_waiters + capacity + sender_count + receiver_dropped atomics
memory_layout.RuntimeStats: Multiple AtomicU64 counters for coroutine counts, switches, I/O bytes

specs.cache_line_analysis: AsyncMutex locked field is hot on every lock attempt. Channel queue mutex is contention point under high throughput. Stats counters are write-heavy, may benefit from per-thread aggregation.

implementation_phases.name: AsyncMutex with atomic CAS and wait queue

diagrams.title: AsyncMutex vs std::sync::Mutex
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison: std mutex blocks thread (futex wait), async mutex yields coroutine (scheduler runs others).

diagrams.title: AsyncMutex Lock Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: try CAS, if fail add to waiters, yield, when woken try CAS again.

diagrams.title: AsyncMutex Memory Layout
diagrams.description: Byte offsets for locked (AtomicBool), padding, waiters (Mutex<VecDeque>), data (UnsafeCell<T>).

diagrams.title: AsyncMutexGuard Lifecycle
diagrams.description: From lock() acquisition through deref access to Drop triggering release and wake.

diagrams.title: AsyncCondvar Wait Sequence
diagrams.description: From wait(guard) call through add to waiters, drop guard (release mutex), yield, when woken re-lock mutex.

diagrams.title: Producer-Consumer with AsyncQueue
diagrams.description: Producer pushes to queue (waits if full via not_full condvar), consumer pops (waits if empty via not_empty).

diagrams.title: MPSC Channel Architecture
diagrams.description: Multiple senders, single receiver, bounded queue, recv_waiters for empty, send_waiters for full.

diagrams.title: Channel Send Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: check receiver_dropped, lock queue, if full add to send_waiters and yield, else push and notify receiver.

diagrams.title: Channel Recv Algorithm
diagrams.description: Step-by-step: lock queue, if empty check sender_count, if 0 return Disconnected, else add to recv_waiters and yield.

diagrams.title: Channel Drop Detection
diagrams.description: Sender drop decrements sender_count, if 0 wakes all receivers. Receiver drop sets flag, wakes all senders.

diagrams.title: Wait-For Graph for Deadlock Detection
diagrams.description: Nodes are coroutines and resources. Edge from coroutine to resource = waiting. Edge from resource to coroutine = held. Cycle = deadlock.

diagrams.title: Deadlock Detection Algorithm
diagrams.description: DFS from waiting coroutine: follow wait edge to resource, follow hold edge to holder, if cycle detected report deadlock chain.

diagrams.title: RuntimeStats Counters
diagrams.description: List of atomic counters: spawned, completed, panicked, runnable, blocked, switches, steals, bytes_read, bytes_written.

diagrams.title: Graceful Shutdown Sequence
diagrams.description: Set shutting_down flag → stop accepting work → drain runnable coroutines → wake blocked coroutines → join workers.

diagrams.title: Performance Benchmark Results Template
diagrams.description: Template for comparing: spawn throughput, channel throughput, mutex throughput, context switch overhead vs Go/Tokio targets.

diagrams.title: Architecture Comparison: Your Runtime vs Go vs Tokio
diagrams.description: Feature matrix comparing stack management, preemption, I/O integration, synchronization primitives, memory per coroutine.

tdd_diagrams_to_generate.title: Architecture Comparison: Your Runtime vs Go vs Tokio
tdd_diagrams_to_generate.description: Feature matrix comparing stack management, preemption, I/O integration, synchronization primitives, memory per coroutine.

criteria: Each coroutine has its own stack (64 KB fixed size with guard page for overflow detection)
Context switch saves/restores all callee-saved registers (rbx, rbp, r12-r15) plus rsp and rip per x86-64 System V ABI
Context switch maintains 16-byte stack alignment (rsp % 16 == 8 after simulated CALL)
Coroutines can yield control voluntarily via yield_now() which switches back to scheduler context
Coroutines can return values on completion (stored in Coroutine struct before state transitions to Finished)
Stack overflow detection implemented via PROT_NONE guard page at stack base
Works on x86-64 Linux with assembly context_switch function
Basic benchmark shows context switch overhead < 100 ns in release mode
Coroutine state machine supports Runnable/Running/Finished/Panicked transitions
Panic handling prevents unwinding across FFI boundary via catch_unwind wrapper
Global queue implemented with Mutex<VecDeque> for new coroutines and overflow handoff
Per-thread local queues using lock-free Chase-Lev SPMC deque implementation
Work-stealing triggered when local queue is empty, stealing half from victim
Configurable number of worker threads via Scheduler::new(num_workers)
Random victim selection using per-thread PRNG to reduce contention patterns
No deadlock under any scheduling pattern - spin/yield/sleep progression prevents worker starvation
Scaling efficiency > 70% measured via parallel benchmark up to CPU core count
LIFO pop from local queue for cache locality, FIFO steal from remote end for fairness
Proper memory ordering with SeqCst fences to prevent race conditions
Cache line padding to prevent false sharing between bottom and top indices
Graceful shutdown with Condvar notification to wake parked workers
Coroutine state transitions: Runnable → Running → Runnable/Finished/Blocked handled correctly
Timer-based preemption flag is set after configurable time slice (default 10ms)
Signal handler sets preemption flag using only async-signal-safe operations (atomic store, no malloc/printf)
Coroutines check preemption flag at safe points via yield_if_preempted() or cooperative_yield()
Preempted coroutines are returned to run queue with Runnable state
Time slice enforcement provides fair scheduling: no coroutine starves for more than (time_slice * coroutines_per_thread)
Preemption check overhead is < 3 cycles per check (atomic load + predicted branch)
Total preemption mechanism overhead is < 1% of execution time for typical workloads
Signal handler does not call non-async-signal-safe functions (validated by code inspection)
Preemption flag is cleared before resuming a coroutine to prevent immediate re-preemption
Context switch during preemption preserves all callee-saved registers per x86-64 ABI
